title: Sinbad and the Giant Bird
content:
Sinbad the Sailor was known throughout Baghdad as the greatest adventurer who ever lived. He had sailed to the edges of the world and returned with tales so extraordinary that people could hardly believe them. This is the story of his second voyage.

On his second journey, Sinbad's ship stopped at a beautiful island that seemed uninhabited. The sailors spread out to explore. Sinbad wandered alone, fell asleep under a tree, and woke to find the ship gone. His companions had sailed away without him.

Sinbad climbed the tallest hill and spotted something enormous and perfectly white in the distance, shaped like a dome. Up close, it was smooth and seamless, taller than a house, with no doors or cracks anywhere.

As Sinbad circled the white dome, the sky grew dark. He looked up and saw a tremendous bird blotting out the sun. It was a Roc, a creature so immense it could carry elephants in its talons. The white dome was the Roc's egg!

Sinbad formed a daring plan. When the Roc landed on its nest, he unwound his turban and tied one end to his waist and the other to the great bird's leg. When morning came, the Roc launched into the sky, and Sinbad soared with it, high above the clouds.

The Roc finally descended into a deep valley. Sinbad untied himself quickly. He looked around in wonder. The ground of the valley glittered and sparkled. It was covered entirely in diamonds! But there was no way in or out, and enormous serpents coiled in every shadowed corner.

Sinbad remembered old merchants' tales about this place. They would throw fresh meat down into the valley. Diamonds would stick to the meat, and eagles would carry it up to their clifftop nests. The merchants would then chase the eagles away and collect the fallen gems.

Sinbad tied as many diamonds as he could carry to a large piece of meat. Then he lay on top of the meat and held on with both hands, squeezing his eyes shut.

A great eagle swept down and carried the meat and Sinbad up to the clifftop. Startled merchants rushed over and found a man instead of just gems. Sinbad laughed and told his story. With his pockets full of diamonds, he found passage home to Baghdad, richer than ever, with the most astonishing tale of all.
